questions.q: Which of these is one of the UNIX/Linux philosophy points listed by the guide?
options: Configure everything through a graphical interface
Prefer one large program over many small ones
questions.why: The list: treat everything as a file; value portability; do one thing and do it well; KISS - Keep It Simple Stupid.

questions.q: "UNIX is a simple operating system, but you have to be a genius to understand the simplicity." Who said it?
questions.why: The guide pairs it with the 'user-friendly... just isn't promiscuous about which users it's friendly with' quip.

questions.q: The guide defines a Linux distribution as...
options: a consistent set of software assembled around the Linux kernel, ready to install with the components to manage itself
any computer that has the Linux kernel installed
the kernel plus exactly one desktop environment
a commercial support contract for Linux
questions.why: Installation, removal, configuration - the distribution ships the management machinery too.

questions.q: Which pairing of distribution types matches the guide?
options: Associative/community: Debian, Rocky - commercial: Red Hat, Ubuntu
Community: Red Hat, Ubuntu - commercial: Debian, Rocky
Community: Arch, Fedora - commercial: Debian, Gentoo
questions.why: Also by audience: beginners (Ubuntu, Mint), advanced users (Gentoo, Arch), servers (Debian, Red Hat), workstations (Fedora).

questions.q: Which of these are named as graphic (desktop) environments?
questions.why: Their ergonomics hold their own against Microsoft or Apple systems; KDE is built on Qt/C++, GNOME on GTK+.
